topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and old bark hit me first, a familiar comfort from childhood summers. I walked down the familiar lane, the one I used to race my friends down, and stopped dead. Where the towering oak, my proud, clumsy creation from when I was eight, had stood sentinel by the fence, there was only a ragged, splintered stump. The sight stole the air from my lungs. My hands, which had been gripping the strap of my backpack so tightly I could feel the strain in my knuckles, began to tremble uncontrollably. I took a stumbling step closer, my boots crunching on loose gravel. The silence of the clearing, usually filled with the rustle of leaves, now felt vast and accusing. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and brittle in the stillness. I knelt down, touching the rough, exposed roots where the trunk had been, a cold dread pooling in my stomach. It was gone. Vanished.

[story 2]

I found the clearing by accident while trimming the overgrown hedge. The space where the sapling used to be—my first attempt at nurturing something green—was brutally bare. It wasn't just a cleared patch; it was an amputation. The raw edges of the soil looked disturbed, as if something immense and careless had swept through. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim slightly at the edges. I stood frozen, my breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. My gaze darted from the freshly cut stumps to the empty space, my jaw tightening until my teeth ached. I could feel a sudden, intense pressure behind my eyes, a desperate need to turn and flee, but my feet remained rooted to the spot. I slowly raised a hand, flexing my fingers as if checking if they were still attached to reality.

[story 3]

It happened on a Tuesday afternoon. I was walking past the old boundary line, a spot I hadn't thought about in years, when I saw it. The imprint of the tree—a sturdy, youthful thing I’d painstakingly watered and protected—was gone. In its place, only muddy earth and the stark evidence of heavy machinery remained. A sudden, sharp intake of breath hitched in my throat, and I stumbled backward, tripping over a root. I landed hard on the uneven ground, the impact jarring my entire frame. I pushed myself up, my hands flying instinctively to cover my mouth, stifling a sound that threatened to escape. My eyes scanned the devastation, seeing only the stark, brutal removal of something I had poured my own hope into. The world seemed to tilt; everything felt suddenly sharp and wrong.
